title: What kind of Rapper I would be   Request

enrichment.summary: Kyle responds to a viewer request about what kind of rapper he would be, connecting rap to the ancient Greek and Roman tradition of oratory, and describing his ideal style as a philosophical 'preacher rapper' who distills ideas into passionate, historically resonant songs. He draws parallels between rhetorical gesticulation, memorized speeches, and modern rap battles.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Rap is modern oratory — its highest form is distilling complex ideas and philosophy into passionate, memorable songs that can become historical teaching tools
Would be a 'preacher rapper' or orator in the Roman tradition, using gesticulations, delivering philosophical speeches set to perfect beats
Chasing a catchy hook limits how deep you can go artistically — the real goal is a perfectly crafted message song
Prefers the political, substantive Philippic style of debate over petty personal clowning, imagining presidential-style rap debates
You can't force it — you have to be ready intellectually and emotionally, then wait for the muse

entities: Aaron Burr (misidentified as Lincoln's debate opponent)

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: If I were to become a rapper, my rap style would be influenced by oratory and history.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: When stoned, my inner monologue sometimes freestyles.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Being a rapper is my whole deal, my identity.

claims.subject: oratory
claims.claim: Oratory was one of the most important skills in ancient Roman and Greek history.

claims.subject: oratory
claims.claim: Ancient orators used sign language and comedic timing to enhance their speeches.

claims.subject: oratory
claims.claim: Good oratory required passion, strong voice, and memorization of speeches.

claims.subject: ancient Greece
claims.claim: Ancient Greece was not an organized kingdom but a collection of tribes.

claims.subject: ancient Greece
claims.claim: Orators could rise to leadership by convincing people through speeches.

claims.subject: oratory
claims.claim: A famous Greek orator regained his stolen inheritance by mastering oratory.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: Modern rappers are like ancient orators, trying to convey messages through their art.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: Rapping evolves from personal topics to deeper messages over time.

claims.subject: music
claims.claim: Rhymes help people remember songs and messages, just like in ancient times.

claims.subject: music
claims.claim: Songs with deep messages can become legendary and teach history.

claims.subject: music
claims.claim: The Flight of the Valkyries caused a frenzy when first played, like a rock concert.

claims.subject: music
claims.claim: Music combines intellect (words) and emotion (delivery).

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: You don’t need a good singing voice to be a rapper, just clear pronunciation.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: Older rappers can still perform well if their minds are sharp.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: Perfect songs require years of research and refinement.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: An album can tell a complete message, with each song leading to the next.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: I’d like to be a 'preacher rapper,' combining ancient oratory with modern rap.

claims.subject: ancient Rome
claims.claim: Ancient Roman orators used specific gesticulations during speeches.

claims.subject: rapping
claims.claim: Rap battles could replace political debates in the future.

claims.subject: artists
claims.claim: Artists like George Michael lose their creative spark when they 'don’t feel it' anymore.

claims.subject: artists
claims.claim: The muse (inspiration) can leave suddenly, just like it can return.

claims.subject: music
claims.claim: Music is called 'music' because it comes from the muse.
